we are going to be moving into graphs and making the comparison between the guideline and the actual facts of the case

nodes.label: Expert Profile Sync (Create/Update/Delete)

nodes.label: Clinical Case Pseudonymization

provenance.quote: we want to evaluate how they're doing with this pseudonymization because they didn't go into too much detail as to how they're going to solve it

nodes.notes: Cumulus tasked with this; concern they will use LLM without rigorous evaluation; requires ground truth annotations to validate

nodes.label: Ground Truth Collection for Pseudonymization Evaluation

provenance.quote: we want to evaluate how they're doing with this pseudonymization ... we need to have annotations or like countries so they can compare what they predicted versus what it should actually be
the starting to collect the ground truth, that's actually the most important one because it will take time for clinicians to do it, so the sooner that we have the stuff for them, the better.

nodes.notes: Steps: select cases (deceased patients, simple, low guidelines/handwriting), define entities with Brendan, get clinical team to annotate, optionally pre-populate with AI; need annotation tool meeting data privacy requirements; identified as highest-priority item

nodes.label: Clinical Entity Extraction

provenance.quote: at the bottom is what we have from the case itself, the facts of the case ... extracting the entities and making the pseudonymization
the most important part ... just go with the entity extraction, because that's the most important part

nodes.notes: Core deliverable from Cumulus green box; entities from clinical case records; same entity types expected in guidelines

nodes.label: NICE API Access and Compliance

have to wait for them to come back to us with the new proposal, which should be this week

nodes.notes: Original proposal exceeded budget; green box (data ingestion + entity extraction + pseudonymization) proposed as reduced scope; awaiting revised proposal

they'll give us the code and we can even use it for other projects and like the nice guidelines

nodes.notes: Cumulus will deliver code (not a black box); transparent deployment on Medbrief infrastructure; reusable for other projects

nodes.notes: Must meet data privacy requirements; may pre-populate using AI to reduce clinical team effort; several tools exist

nodes.label: Rules Representation (Cypher Queries vs Graphs)

provenance.quote: We can either make them as cipher queries or do them as graphs. Now, the cipher queries is like an intermediate step to get to graphs.

nodes.notes: Mediha demonstrated Cypher query approach at Sprint 9; graph is ultimate goal; requires entity extraction from guidelines as prerequisite

nodes.label: Cumulus Two-Track Pipeline Architecture

provenance.quote: those are two completely independent separate pipelines of processing, and then they provide the guideline structuring based on what they will receive from the NICE API, and then then the whole entity extraction and all that stuff based on the case data

nodes.notes: Track 1 (top): NICE guideline ingestion and structuring. Track 2 (bottom): clinical case entity extraction and pseudonymization. Independent; merged at reasoning/graph layer

nodes.notes: Unresolved at time of asking; resolved later in chapter 5 (Deon takes it)

nodes.label: What annotation tool should be used for ground truth collection that meets data privacy requirements?

provenance.quote: we have to make sure that they meet like data privacy and our politics, or if we want to do it ourselves, how to do it, basically defining how we're going to do that

nodes.notes: Open question; options include existing tools or building in-house

nodes.label: How does Mediha's graph database work fit alongside Cumulus's proposed pipeline?

provenance.quote: What is Mediha taking next steps on her stuff, and then with getting figuring out where Cumulus can fit in?

nodes.notes: Laura answered by showing Cumulus diagram; Mediha currently on leave so no immediate next steps

nodes.label: Is it compliant to share the NICE API key with Cumulus (non-UK based)?

provenance.quote: I don't feel that comfortable with giving them the API key. I don't know what you think, Deon, because they're not based in the UK. So I think we might not be compliant.

nodes.notes: Unresolved; to be investigated with Brendan; Deon leans toward it being acceptable with key rotation

nodes.label: Should Medbrief do NICE guideline extraction before or after the Cumulus engagement, to potentially reuse their pipeline?

provenance.quote: do we do this before cumulus or after cumulus, our nice guideline extraction, so that we could use their pipeline basically. Once they do the handover.

nodes.notes: Raised by Deon; unresolved; depends on Cumulus scope clarification

nodes.label: Is there any additional Cumulus material available beyond the pre-hackathon documentation?

provenance.quote: Is there any more, I know there's the video from yesterday that we're going to request access to. Beyond that, all I've got from Cumulus is I think the documentation that you shared with me, Laura... So is there anything more to share that's useful?

nodes.notes: Partially answered: presentation exists but not yet accessible

nodes.label: Should the human-in-the-loop / thumbs up-down work be Deon's or should Rowan also be involved?

provenance.quote: I don't know if that's something that you would take, Deon, or should we have Rowan look into it as well?

nodes.notes: Resolved: stays with Deon; Rowan may have collation role later

nodes.label: Expert Match endpoint task requires significant prior knowledge which may make it unsuitable for Rowan at this stage

provenance.quote: I'm just worried that it's going to take a lot of prior knowledge to execute on this potentially.

nodes.notes: Deon's concern; reason task remains tentatively with Deon pending priority review

nodes.label: Cumulus's approach to pseudonymization is vague; they likely do not yet have a firm plan

provenance.quote: I think they don't know what they're going to do, to be honest. ... he said he, John says he comes up with suggestions and then the data scientists suggest other stuff.

nodes.notes: Laura and Deon's shared assessment; reason for insisting on ground truth evaluation framework

nodes.label: Without ground truth, third-party vendors produce proof-of-concept work of unknown quality

provenance.quote: if you don't give them ground truth, they'll just do like a kind of proof of concept thing that you don't even know if it works well or not. So you end up doing everything again.

nodes.notes: Laura's experience with third parties; justification for ground truth collection work

nodes.label: NICE guideline data is effectively publicly available (accessible on NICE website), reducing compliance concern

provenance.quote: it's publicly available data or it's like fairly available data. Or you can look up all the nice guidelines like on their website. This is just an API form of it.

nodes.notes: Deon's view; used to support sharing API key temporarily

nodes.label: Cumulus will deliver code transparently and Medbrief engineers can extend and reuse it

provenance.quote: they'll give us the code and we can even use it for other projects and like the nice guidelines. ... they were actually quite deliberate in saying that because they were like happy that we had engineers

nodes.label: Third parties often recommend the newest or most expensive Azure services regardless of suitability

provenance.quote: they end up giving us a service that's like the newest one that Azure has in the market because they want to have more use cases with that or the one that's more costly or whatever and you end up having something that it's not scalable or you don't even know how to use it

nodes.label: The team lacks data engineering expertise and cannot critically evaluate Cumulus's data architecture claims

provenance.quote: we don't have anyone in the team that has a data engineering formation. So, if they tell us we're using Databricks and Azure Data Factory and this lake, we just believe them
with the AI side, I feel comfortable. But with the data side, they could just be selling us whatever.

nodes.label: Rowan is not a data engineering expert and has not used these tools himself

provenance.quote: I'm also not a data engineering expert and so building these tools I have not used myself.

nodes.label: Rowan having time to investigate the data engineering stack is the most important thing his team is currently lacking

provenance.quote: just having that time to look into it is what your team is lacking at the moment, Laoura

nodes.label: Azure Key Vault (proposed NICE API key storage)
nodes.text: Deon proposes storing the NICE API key in Azure Key Vault so Cumulus can use it programmatically without being able to view or extract it, as a potential compliance mitigation.

provenance.quote: If you pop that key in the key vault so that they can't view it, but they can use it, that's possible, right, with Azure as far as I know. So they're going to use it in our infrastructure.

nodes.label: NICE may charge non-UK users for API access, adding a financial compliance dimension
nodes.statement: Laura asserts NICE charges users outside the UK, which would make sharing the key with Cumulus (non-UK based) a compliance and financial issue beyond just access eligibility.

provenance.quote: they do have, I think they charge people that are outside of the UK or something like that. So I think. We might want to look into that with Brendan

nodes.label: Entity types in clinical guidelines and clinical case records are expected to be the same
nodes.statement: Laura states that the entity types Cumulus would extract from clinical case records will likely be identical to those needed from NICE guidelines, making the two pipeline tracks complementary rather than divergent.

provenance.quote: we need them to be able to extract the entities from the guidelines, which are going to be probably the same entities as the ones in the clinical records.

nodes.label: Cumulus planned to focus on a narrow case type (e.g. obstetrics) to limit the NICE guidelines scope
nodes.statement: Deon recalls Cumulus described wanting to focus on a particular clinical speciality (citing obstetrics as an example) in order to reduce the number of NICE guidelines they would need to query or process.

provenance.quote: they also said they wanted to focus on a particular kind of case, like obstetrics or whatever, and I think that was also to reduce the number of nice guidelines that they would potentially need to query or look up.

nodes.label: Cumulus will reveal more about their technical approach once the engagement formally starts
nodes.statement: Deon reports that Cumulus stated they will share and discuss more detail about their planned implementation once the engagement has formally begun, giving Medbrief input at that stage.

provenance.quote: once we start the engagement, they're going to reveal more, discuss more about how they're planning to do stuff, so that we also have input on that.

nodes.label: Only two cases are needed for the initial ground truth annotation exercise
nodes.statement: Laura states that Cumulus indicated only two cases are required for the initial ground truth dataset, implying the annotation effort is manageable in scope.

provenance.quote: They said they only needed two, so it shouldn't be that difficult.

nodes.label: Clinicians cannot annotate ground truth cases during their billable working hours
nodes.statement: Laura notes a practical scheduling constraint: clinicians cannot perform ground truth annotation at the same time as their paid clinical work, so annotation must happen after their shifts end.

provenance.quote: we can't do it at the same time because they're in their billable hours, when they're done with that, they'll be able to look into the thumbs down messages and give us a bit more of detail as to why it was incorrect

nodes.label: Deon to investigate anomalous expert profile instances created via direct profile update (scheduled for Friday)
nodes.text: Deon identifies that some experts have been created through the system via direct profile updates, which should not be possible. He was scheduled to investigate these instances on Friday but had not yet done so at time of meeting.

provenance.quote: some experts have been created through the system, which where they've gone in and updated their profiles, which shouldn't be the case, so I'll need to have a look at those. I'm meant to do it on Friday, but I don't have time why those instances have been created, but anyway.

nodes.label: Data architecture review is worthwhile regardless of whether Cumulus is ultimately engaged
nodes.statement: Even if Medbrief does not accept Cumulus's proposal, reviewing their proposed data architecture is still valuable because Medbrief will need to build the same stack with or without Cumulus.

provenance.quote: even if we don't, I think it's still useful to look into this because we are still going to build it with them or without them. So we still need to look into it.

nodes.label: Expert Match AI ingestion endpoint — three-table structure (expert, case, case-expert)
nodes.text: Laura specifies that the ingestion endpoint covers three distinct tables: an expert table, a case table, and a case-expert relationship table. This structural detail is load-bearing for understanding which events must trigger a sync call.

provenance.quote: you'll see that we have an expert table, a case table, and a case expert table. And that's something that you can see from the endpoint.

nodes.label: Medbrief could ingest NICE guidelines itself and supply the resulting JSON directly to Cumulus, avoiding the API key sharing problem
nodes.statement: Laura proposes an alternative to giving Cumulus the NICE API key: Medbrief performs the NICE ingestion internally and then hands Cumulus the structured JSON output, so Cumulus never needs direct API access.

provenance.quote: we might want to do that ourselves and just give them the output of the queries, like exactly how we got the result from nice, but just giving them the JSONs.

nodes.label: Cumulus quoted the engagement at 10 weeks; Deon's internal estimate is 6 to 8 weeks
nodes.statement: During the NICE API key discussion Deon references the engagement duration as six to eight weeks, but notes that Cumulus themselves stated ten weeks, indicating a discrepancy in timeline expectations.

provenance.quote: for the duration of the work, so for the six to 8 weeks, I know they say 10 weeks, but for the duration of the work

nodes.label: The safest Cumulus scope is entity extraction only; NICE guideline processing should be excluded from the current engagement
nodes.statement: Deon recommends that the safest path is to remove NICE guideline work from the Cumulus scope entirely and focus only on entity extraction from case data, because Cumulus's intentions for the guidelines are unclear and entity extraction is the most important deliverable.

provenance.quote: the safest path again here, like Laoura described, would be to avoid the NICE guideline in this instance and just go with the entity extraction, because that's the most important part. Because we, I have no idea what they're playing with the the nice guidelines according to this either.

nodes.label: Rowan working with other devs on Expert Match would create distraction, justifying keeping him focused on Cumulus work
nodes.statement: Beyond the prior-knowledge concern (claim:0001), Deon adds a second distinct reason for retaking the Expert Match task: requiring Rowan to coordinate closely with other developers would pull him away from the focused data engineering investigation the team most needs from him right now.

provenance.quote: needing to like work too much with the other devs on stuff like that might just distract the situation. So I'll take that one for the team for this for now.

nodes.label: Explicit priority order: ground truth collection first (clinician time is bottleneck), data strategy last (longer-term)
nodes.statement: Laura states the priority ordering explicitly at the close of the meeting: starting ground truth collection is the single most important item because it depends on clinician availability and will take the most calendar time; the data strategy work is deprioritised as a longer-horizon task.

provenance.quote: I think there's things that are more priority than others, like obviously the data strategy is something that will take more time, but the starting to collect the ground truth, that's actually the most important one because it will take time for clinicians to do it, so the sooner that we have the stuff for them, the better.

nodes.label: Expert Match Storage Endpoint Integration
nodes.summary: Laura opens with a request for the dev team to call the AI ingestion endpoint whenever an expert or case is created, updated, or deleted — removing the need for manual sync. The team discusses the update queue table, cache invalidation, and whether this task should sit with Deon or Rowan.

nodes.label: Cumulus Scope and Pseudonymization Ground Truth
nodes.summary: Laura introduces the Cumulus partnership scope: entity extraction and pseudonymization. The team agrees they need to create their own ground truth (via clinician annotation of deceased-patient cases) to evaluate Cumulus's output, and discusses tooling for annotation and pre-population with AI to reduce clinician burden.

nodes.label: Cumulus Flow Diagram and NICE API Compliance
nodes.summary: Laura shares her screen showing Cumulus's proposed pipeline: guideline structuring (NICE) and case entity extraction running in parallel before feeding a reasoning layer. The team debates NICE API key sharing, compliance (UK-based requirement), and whether to let Cumulus use the key via Azure Key Vault or to ingest NICE guidelines internally and hand Cumulus the JSON output.

nodes.label: Data Architecture Review and Rowan's Onboarding
nodes.summary: Laura asks Rowan to review Cumulus's data architecture proposal (Databricks, Azure Data Factory, Data Lake) and assess whether it is fit for purpose, citing concerns about vendors upselling costly or non-scalable services. Deon suggests using the enterprise skills development platform. Rowan is candid about his limited data engineering tool experience but commits to applying his judgement.

nodes.label: Priorities, Human-in-the-Loop, and Closing Decisions
nodes.summary: The team wraps up remaining items: Sally integration stays with Deon; human-in-the-loop (thumbs up/down for Ask Alex chat) is Dave's ticket but Rowan may help with data collation later; Deon takes back the storage endpoint task after assessing it requires too much context for Rowan to ramp on right now. Final priority order: Cumulus ground truth, data architecture review, then ongoing sprint work.

Deon Kuhn and Rowan Reid are visible in side-panel thumbnails.

nodes.description: Chapter-marker frame showing the kumulus_flow.png architecture diagram still on screen with Deon Kuhn highlighted as the active speaker (blue name badge), alongside Laura Gongas and Rowan Reid in the side panel. This marks the start of a new chapter section within the diagram discussion.

edges.notes: Ch5 decision (Rowan focuses on Cumulus; Deon retakes Expert Match) directly answers Ch1 open question about who owns the endpoint task

source_quote.quote: the ingestion endpoints for the experts when the data update, I'm going to say I'm going to do that rather
source_quote.speaker: Deon Kuhn

target_quote.quote: I don't know if we should forward this task to Rowan, or is it something that you're already working on?
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch1 claim that the Expert Match task requires too much prior knowledge directly backs the Ch5 decision to keep Rowan off it and focus him on Cumulus

source_quote.quote: I'm just worried that it's going to take a lot of prior knowledge to execute on this potentially.
source_quote.speaker: Deon Kuhn

target_quote.quote: I'll take that one for the team for this for now
target_quote.speaker: Deon Kuhn

edges.notes: Ch2 claim that without ground truth third-party vendors produce PoC work of unknown quality directly backs the Ch4 prioritisation of ground truth collection as the team's most important current item

source_quote.quote: if you don't give them ground truth, they'll just do like a kind of proof of concept thing that you don't even know if it works well or not. So you end up doing everything again.
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

target_quote.quote: the starting to collect the ground truth, that's actually the most important one because it will take time for clinicians to do it, so the sooner that we have the stuff for them, the better.
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch2 claim that Cumulus has not settled on a pseudonymization approach reinforces the Ch4 decision to make ground truth collection the top priority (so evaluation can happen independently of Cumulus's choices)

source_quote.quote: I think they don't know what they're going to do, to be honest. ... he said he, John says he comes up with suggestions and then the data scientists suggest other stuff.
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

target_quote.quote: the starting to collect the ground truth, that's actually the most important one because it will take time for clinicians to do it, so the sooner that we have the stuff for them, the better.
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch3 claim that Cumulus will deliver code transparently motivates the Ch4 action for Rowan to review the architecture proposal (code ownership makes the architecture choice consequential for Medbrief's long-term maintainability)

source_quote.quote: they'll give us the code and we can even use it for other projects and like the nice guidelines. ... they were actually quite deliberate in saying that because they were like happy that we had engineers
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

target_quote.quote: perhaps you could look into their proposal, do a bit of research and see if it makes sense to do it that way or not
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch5 action for Rowan to watch the hackathon recording cannot proceed until Ch2 action for Laura to email the recording access request is completed

source_quote.quote: we'll wait until we get this recording as well. That will be useful. Then I can watch that.
source_quote.speaker: Rowan Reid

target_quote.quote: Yeah, so maybe we should ask for that. Okay, I'll send them an email.
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch4 action for Rowan to review the Cumulus architecture proposal is gated on Ch4 action for Laura to forward the presentation once it becomes available — Rowan explicitly notes the material gap

source_quote.quote: I'm absolutely keen to apply my mind to those.
source_quote.speaker: Rowan Reid

target_quote.quote: once we have it, I'll make sure to forward it to you
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch4 claim that the team lacks data engineering expertise and cannot critically evaluate Cumulus's stack directly motivates the Ch4 action for Rowan (the most technically adjacent person) to conduct the architecture review

source_quote.quote: we don't have anyone in the team that has a data engineering formation. So, if they tell us we're using Databricks and Azure Data Factory and this lake, we just believe them
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

target_quote.quote: perhaps you could look into their proposal, do a bit of research and see if it makes sense to do it that way or not
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch4 claim that third parties recommend expensive/newest Azure services regardless of fit provides direct motivation for the Ch4 action to independently review the Cumulus stack

source_quote.quote: they end up giving us a service that's like the newest one that Azure has in the market because they want to have more use cases with that or the one that's more costly or whatever and you end up having something that it's not scalable or you don't even know how to use it
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

target_quote.quote: perhaps you could look into their proposal, do a bit of research and see if it makes sense to do it that way or not
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: The Brendan meeting (action:0003, invited in Ch2, confirmed again in Ch5) is positioned as the step that will clarify how Mediha's graph database work fits with the Cumulus pipeline, addressing the open Ch2 question

source_quote.quote: I've added you to a meeting tomorrow. you can attend to that. Rowan: I can, I can make it for that work, yeah, so I'll join that.
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas / Rowan Reid

target_quote.quote: What is Mediha taking next steps on her stuff, and then with getting figuring out where Cumulus can fit in?
target_quote.speaker: Rowan Reid

edges.notes: Brendan's patent document (Ch2), which describes the full system and breaks it into modules including entity identification and pseudonymization, provides the conceptual foundation and entity-type reference that ground truth annotation work will need to operationalise

source_quote.quote: compare it back to the patent, that Brendan's patent document, and he had that description of like the whole system, then we had broken it down into different modules
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

target_quote.quote: we want to evaluate how they're doing with this pseudonymization ... we need to have annotations or like countries so they can compare what they predicted versus what it should actually be
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch3 tentative decision (share NICE API key with Cumulus temporarily, rotate key afterwards) directly addresses the Ch3 open compliance question about whether sharing with a non-UK party is acceptable

source_quote.quote: if you give them access during the time of development and just rotate the key once they're done, I think that should be fine
source_quote.speaker: Deon Kuhn

target_quote.quote: I don't feel that comfortable with giving them the API key. I don't know what you think, Deon, because they're not based in the UK. So I think we might not be compliant.
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch3 claim that NICE guideline data is effectively public (available on the NICE website) directly backs the Ch3 tentative decision to share the API key with Cumulus temporarily (reduced compliance concern)

source_quote.quote: it's publicly available data or it's like fairly available data. Or you can look up all the nice guidelines like on their website. This is just an API form of it.
source_quote.speaker: Deon Kuhn

target_quote.quote: if you give them access during the time of development and just rotate the key once they're done, I think that should be fine
target_quote.speaker: Deon Kuhn

edges.notes: Mediha's graph database work (Ch2, presented at Sprint 9) provides the concrete artefact that grounds the Ch3 rules representation discussion (Cypher queries as intermediate step toward the graph)

source_quote.quote: Mediha presented at the Sprint Nine review the work that she had been doing on the kind of the graph database side of things in terms of represent, like once we extract all the entities and the relationships
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

target_quote.quote: We can either make them as cipher queries or do them as graphs. Now, the cipher queries is like an intermediate step to get to graphs.
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch3 rules representation work (building Cypher queries or graphs from guidelines) explicitly depends on entity extraction from guidelines being completed first — it is described as the downstream layer

source_quote.quote: We can either make them as cipher queries or do them as graphs. Now, the cipher queries is like an intermediate step to get to graphs.
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

target_quote.quote: the most important part ... just go with the entity extraction, because that's the most important part
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch3 action (team to clarify with Cumulus their intended depth of NICE guideline entity extraction) is the concrete step taken to resolve the Ch3 strategic question of whether to do NICE extraction before or after the Cumulus engagement

source_quote.quote: I think that's a good point to ask them, but to ask them until what point they're going to be involved in the NICE guidelines and what we're going to get out from them.
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

target_quote.quote: do we do this before cumulus or after cumulus, our nice guideline extraction, so that we could use their pipeline basically. Once they do the handover.
target_quote.speaker: Deon Kuhn

edges.notes: Ch4 action for Laura to forward the Cumulus architecture presentation to Rowan once available directly addresses the Ch4 open question about whether any further Cumulus material exists beyond the pre-hackathon docs

source_quote.quote: once we have it, I'll make sure to forward it to you
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

target_quote.quote: Is there any more, I know there's the video from yesterday that we're going to request access to. Beyond that, all I've got from Cumulus is I think the documentation that you shared with me, Laura... So is there anything more to share that's useful?
target_quote.speaker: Rowan Reid

edges.notes: Ch5 decision (thumbs up/down tickets stay with Deon; Rowan may have a collation role later) directly answers the Ch5 open question about whether Deon or Rowan owns the human-in-the-loop feedback work

source_quote.quote: keep it on my side, but I think Rowan would have a role to play potentially just in like collating the data and making sure that the data is coming through correctly
source_quote.speaker: Deon Kuhn

target_quote.quote: I don't know if that's something that you would take, Deon, or should we have Rowan look into it as well?
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: Ch5 claim that having Rowan investigate the data engineering stack is what the team is currently most lacking directly backs the Ch4 action assigning that review to him

source_quote.quote: just having that time to look into it is what your team is lacking at the moment, Laoura
source_quote.speaker: Deon Kuhn

target_quote.quote: perhaps you could look into their proposal, do a bit of research and see if it makes sense to do it that way or not
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

edges.notes: The Ch5 human-in-the-loop strategy (closing the MLOps loop via thumbs up/down feedback) is structurally parallel to and supports the rationale for Ch2 ground truth collection — both are about acquiring labelled signal from clinical experts to measure and improve AI quality

source_quote.quote: we're trying to have human in the loop in most of our projects to basically close the entire MLOps pipeline
source_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas

target_quote.quote: we need to have annotations or like countries so they can compare what they predicted versus what it should actually be
target_quote.speaker: Laura Gongas
